retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mastication Muscles -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The arterial supply to the muscles of mastication is via the maxillary artery, a branch of the external carotid artery.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mastication Muscles -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The primary and accessory muscles of mastication work in a coordinated fashion to produce mandibular movement for chewing food.  The accessory muscles of mastication are the buccinator, suprahyoid muscles (digastric muscle, mylohyoid muscle, and geniohyoid muscle), and infrahyoid muscles (the sternohyoid, sternothyroid, thyrohyoid and omohyoid muscle). [1] [3] The origin, insertion, and action of the main muscles of mastication, as well as a brief description of the accessory muscles of mastication, are as follows.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Cheeks -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Most of the muscles in the cheek region will participate in facial expression except for the buccinator and the masseter muscle. The buccinator muscle is the muscle closes to the oral cavity. The buccinator muscle is the primary muscle that will participate in supporting the food bolus in the mouth during chewing and swallowing. The masseter muscle is the only muscle located in the cheek region and engages in mastication.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Buccinator Muscle -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The buccinator muscle is sometimes referred to as an accessory mastication muscle as it coordinates with the orbicularis oris and superior constrictor muscle during swallowing, mastication, blowing, and sucking. The buccinator compresses the cheek inward against the molars. Some authors propose that the muscle thickens the cheek mucosa, acting as a muscular hydrostat. This thickening enables the cheek to push food boluses toward the tongue. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mastication Muscles -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The primary muscles of mastication (chewing food) are the temporalis, medial pterygoid, lateral pterygoid, and masseter muscles.  The four main muscles of mastication attach to the rami of the mandible and function to move the jaw (mandible).  The cardinal mandibular movements of mastication are elevation, depression, protrusion, retraction, and side to side movement.  To augment the process of eating, the muscles of mastication also move the mandible in a side to side motion to assist in the grinding of food.  The muscles of mastication also function to approximate (bring together or close) the teeth.  The superficial muscle of the neck, the platysma muscle, also assists with depression of the mandible against resistance. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mastication Muscles -- Muscles -- Accessory Muscles of Mastication
retrieved_docs.content: The strap muscles are composed of the suprahyoid, and infrahyoid muscles are located on the side of the neck bilaterally.  The strap muscles primarily function to raise and depress the hyoid bone and larynx.  The strap muscles also assist with depression of the mandible when opening the mouth against an opposing force.  The buccinator is a facial expression muscle that helps in mastication by keeping food pushed back within the oral cavity. [1] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Muscles -- Structure and Function -- Mastication Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The muscles of mastication mechanically break down food into smaller, more digestible pieces. These muscles are primarily located around the jaw and are responsible for the intricate movements of chewing, biting, and grinding food. The primary mastication muscles include the masseter, temporalis, medial and lateral pterygoid, mylohyoid, and digastric muscles.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Masseter Muscle -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The masseter is one of the muscles of mastication. It is a powerful superficial quadrangular muscle originating from the zygomatic arch and inserts along the angle and lateral surface of the mandibular ramus. The masseter is primarily responsible for the elevation of the mandible and some protraction of the mandible. It receives its motor innervation from the mandibular division of the trigeminal nerve. The blood supply is primarily from the masseteric artery, a branch of the internal maxillary artery.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Muscles -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Despite different innervations and functions, the facial muscles, by and large, act synchronously. For example, during chewing, the orbicularis oris and buccinator retain food inside the mouth, while the masseter and temporalis move the mandible when chewing food. The pterygoid muscles move the mandible when the latter needs anteroposterior or lateral and medial adjustment.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mastication Muscles -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The four main muscles of mastication are all innervated by the anterior trunk of the mandibular nerve, which is the third division of the trigeminal nerve (CN V3).  The mandibular nerve (CN V3) is the largest and inferior-most division of the trigeminal nerve (CN V). The trigeminal nerve (CN V) exits the skull via foramen ovale of the greater wing of the sphenoid bone.  The mandibular nerve contains both sensory and motor fibers.  The mandibular nerve is the only division of the trigeminal nerve that carries motor fibers. The mandibular nerve (CN V3) further subdivides as it innervates the four main muscles of mastication.  The temporalis muscle receives innervation by deep temporal branches of the mandibular nerve.  The medial pterygoid muscle receives innervation from the medial pterygoid nerve, a division of the mandibular nerve. The lateral pterygoid muscle gets its nerve supply from the lateral pterygoid nerves, divisions of the mandibular nerve. The masseter muscle receives nerve input from the masseteric nerve, a division of the mandibular nerve. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Buccinator Muscle -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The buccinator, orbicularis oris, and pharyngeal constrictors form a functional unit—the buccinator mechanism—essential to orofacial functions like swallowing, sucking, whistling, chewing, and vowel pronunciation. Food accumulates in the vestibule during chewing if the buccinator is weakened or paralyzed.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Salivation -- Issues of Concern -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The submandibular glands are approximately the size of a walnut and are located under and medial to the mandible of the jaw beneath the floor of the mouth. They, like the parotid glands, contain superficial and deep lobes. The excretory ducts of these glands, also known as Wharton’s ducts, run above the mylohyoid muscle and end at the sublingual caruncle beneath the tongue. Blood supply to the submandibular glands come from branches of the facial and lingual arteries and venous drainage by the submental veins to the facial vein. Nearby structures include the hypoglossal nerve which is important for motor function of the tongue, the lingual nerve which is necessary for the sensory function of the tongue, the marginal mandibular nerve which gives motor function to the muscles that assist in smiling, and the platysma muscle which supports in the movement of the lower lip.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Orbicularis Oris Muscle -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Orbicularis oris muscle along with the buccinator and pharyngeal constrictor form a functional unit, known as "buccinator mechanism" which has an important role in orofacial function (swallowing, sucking, whistling, chewing, vowel pronunciation, kissing). [17] If buccinator and orbicularis oris muscles are weakened or paralyzed, food tends to accumulate in the oral vestibule during chewing.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Swallowing -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Before a food bolus can be propelled toward the pharynx signaling the start of the pharyngeal stage, the muscles of mastication must be recruited to help break down the ingested food. The masseter, temporalis, lateral and medial pterygoid muscles complete this task via innervation from CN V3.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mastication Muscles -- Muscles -- Masseter
retrieved_docs.content: The masseter muscle is a rectangularly shaped muscle with three layers (superficial, deep, and intermediate).  The origin of the masseter muscle is the inferior zygomatic arch and the anterior two-thirds of the zygomatic arch, with a connection to the posterior aspect of the zygomatic bone.  The masseter muscle fibers converge inferiorly, forming a tendon that inserts the outer surface of the mandibular ramus and coronoid process of the mandible.  The function of the masseter muscle is to elevate the mandible and approximate the teeth—additionally, the intermediate and deep muscle fibers of the masseter function to retract the mandible.  And the superficial fibers function to protrude the mandible.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Cheeks -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Deep to the fat pads are the muscles. There are many muscles in the cheek region. The masseter muscle is the largest in the cheek region. The masseter contributes to the lateral fullness of the cheek, but its primary function is mastication. The lower part of the orbicularis oculi muscle contributes to the superior part of the cheek. The levator labii superioris alaeque nasi muscle is on the lateral border of the nose, and it demarcates the medial contour of the cheek region. The muscle lateral to the levator labii superioris alaeque nasi muscle is the levator labii superioris, it is part of the medial cheek region. The zygomaticus minor muscle and the zygomaticus major muscle contributes to the middle cheek region. The zygomaticus major also has some attachment in the superior cheek region. Slightly lower to the zygomaticus muscles, the risorius muscle and the levator anguli oris lays. These two muscles attach to the angle of the mouth. Deep to all these muscles, lies the buccinator muscle. The buccinator muscle’s function is to hold food boluses in the mouth against the teeth during mastication.

Together, these relationships delineate how a diverse spectrum of nervous‑system disorders is organized by their pathogenic mechanisms and anatomical involvement, providing a framework essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 508
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is categorized as a placement of an oral and pharyngeal airway (تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي). Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, and the larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Injection of larynx includes, as a subordinate concept, the placement of an oral and pharyngeal airway, and the broader group of other non‑therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system also contains this airway‑placement procedure. Together, these connections highlight how airway management interventions such as intubation, mechanical ventilation, and oral‑pharyngeal airway placement are organized within the taxonomy of respiratory procedures, with the larynx functioning as a central anatomical focus for both diagnostic and non‑therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2332
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly associated with the larynx, indicating that the larynx is the primary anatomical focus of these examinations. In addition, the larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑OR therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, meaning that it can be involved secondarily in various non‑operative respiratory treatments. Understanding that the larynx functions both as the main target for diagnostic evaluation of the larynx and trachea and as an indirect site for additional respiratory therapies is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care.

Collectively, these procedures demonstrate that Excision functions as a versatile operative strategy adaptable to endocrine, hepatobiliary, neurosurgical, spinal, musculoskeletal, urologic, breast, and craniofacial contexts, supporting comprehensive patient assessment, accurate coding, and optimized multidisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1263
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly linked to the larynx, indicating that examinations and assessments targeting these structures focus on the laryngeal anatomy. The relationship identified as “indirect_procedure_site_of” connects the larynx to the broader category “other non-or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system,” meaning that the larynx can serve as an indirect site for non‑operative therapeutic interventions within the respiratory system. Within this category, the relationship “has child” designates the specific procedure “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” (placement of an oral and pharyngeal airway) as a subordinate or child procedure. Consequently, the placement of an oral/pharyngeal airway is classified under “other non-or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system” and may involve the larynx indirectly. Recognizing these interconnected relationships aids clinicians in coordinating diagnostic evaluations of the larynx with therapeutic airway management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 984
retrieved_docs.content: Neuromuscular disease (اعتلال عصبي عضلي) is connected to paralysis (شلل), indicating that disorders affecting both nerves and muscles can result in loss of motor function. Muscle disorder (اضطراب العضلات) also has a relationship with paralysis, reinforcing the link between impaired muscle function and the development of paralysis. Other disorders of synovium, tendon, and bursa are related to muscle disorder, suggesting that pathological changes in joint‑related structures can influence muscle health and thereby contribute to paralytic outcomes. More specific conditions, such as lesion in the sciatic nerve (آفة في العصب الوركي) and facial nerve disorder (اضطراب العصب الوجهي), are identified as narrower relationships to paralysis, highlighting that focal nerve injuries or dysfunctions are precise causes of paralytic deficits. Together, these concepts illustrate how a spectrum of neuromuscular, muscular, and peripheral nerve pathologies are interrelated and can culminate in paralysis, underscoring the importance of evaluating both muscle and nerve integrity in patients presenting with motor impairment.

Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

This framework supports precise anatomical localization, differential diagnosis, and therapeutic planning, especially the central role of nerve decompression procedures across multiple anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.content: عضلي), Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease (داء شاركو‑ماري‑توث), Bell’s palsy (شلل بل), phantom‑limb syndrome (متلازمة الطرف الشبحي), horseshoe‑tail syndrome with neurogenic bladder (متلازمة ذنب الفرس مع مثانة عصبية), Willis‑muscle weakness (وهن عضلي وبيل), lower‑limb reflexive sclerosis (الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي), acute infectious bronchitis (التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي), single‑nerve neuritis (التهاب العصب الأحادي) and multi‑single‑nerve neuritis (التهاب العصب الأحادي المتعدد). Peripheral nerves (periferní nervy) are the primary anatomic locus for the majority of these conditions, including Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease, muscular neuropathy, all forms of polyneuropathy, horseshoe‑tail syndrome, trigeminal neuralgia pain and Bell’s palsy. Damage to peripheral nerves of the thorax represents a specific manifestation of peripheral neuropathy, and Neurologic qualifiers extend to alcoholic polyneuropathy, trigeminal neuralgia pain and Bell’s palsy. Common clinical findings across

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of the nervous system is a broad diagnostic category that includes a variety of distinct neurological conditions. It is classified as تكزز, as اعتلال الأعصاب المترقي مجهول السبب (idiopathic progressive neuropathy), as اعتلال الأعصاب المتعدد في المرض الوعائي الكولاجيني (multifocal neuropathy associated with collagen vascular disease), as التهاب الشعب الهوائية الحاد المعدي (acute infectious bronchitis), as التهاب العصب الأحادي (mononeuritis), as الحثل الانعكاسي الودى للطرف السفلي (reflex sympathetic atrophy of the lower limb), as داء شاركو- ماري- توث (Charcot‑Marie‑Tooth disease), and as وهن عضلي وبيل (muscular and Bell’s palsy). By grouping these diverse entities under the umbrella of other disorders of the nervous system, clinicians can recognize that they share a common classification despite differing pathophysiology, clinical presentation, and management strategies, facilitating comprehensive assessment and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 987
retrieved_docs.content: اعتلال عصبي عضلي (muscle neuropathy) is linked to شلل (paralysis), indicating that neuropathic involvement of muscle tissue can result in loss of motor function. Other disorders of synovium, tendon, and bursa are associated with اضطراب العضلات (muscle disorder), showing that pathological changes in peri‑articular structures often affect muscle integrity. اضطراب العضلات itself has a relationship with شلل, reinforcing the concept that muscle disorders can progress to or coexist with paralysis. اضطراب العصب الوجهي (facial nerve disorder) includes ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي (upper facial atrophy) as a specific manifestation, and it also has a narrower relationship with شلل, suggesting that facial nerve pathology may contribute to localized or generalized motor weakness. آفة في العصب الوركي (lesion in the sciatic nerve) similarly has a narrower relationship with شلل, highlighting that focal peripheral nerve lesions can directly produce paralysis of the innervated limb. Collectively, these connections illustrate how neuromuscular and musculoskeletal disorders—ranging from muscle neuropathy and tendon‑synovial disease to facial and sciatic nerve lesions—are interrelated pathways that can culminate in paralysis, underscoring the importance of comprehensive evaluation and management of both nerve and muscle pathology in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx and are organized as a subcategory of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum. According to the indirect_procedure_site_of relationship, the larynx also serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. This dual classification highlights the larynx’s central role both as the primary anatomical focus of specific diagnostic interventions and as a secondary site affected by broader non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, emphasizing its importance in the overall assessment and management of respiratory tract conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 920
retrieved_docs.content: Dantrolene and its derivatives are classified as directly acting muscle relaxants, situating them within the broader category of muscle‑relaxant agents that exert their therapeutic effect by acting directly on skeletal muscle tissue.
